channel.title: The Crash Report

item.title: The Cadillac CT5 Got Five Stars and Four 'Poors.' Both Are Correct.
item.description: The 2026 Cadillac CT5 holds NHTSA's highest safety rating and IIHS's lowest. GM cited the stars to dismiss the failures. The consumer loses either way.
item.author: Vin Wreckage

item.title: Passenger Deaths Dropped 44% in 30 Years. Driver Deaths Dropped 13%. Nobody Noticed.
item.description: FARS data shows passengers are being saved at 3x the rate of drivers. The driver-to-passenger death ratio shifted from 2.1:1 to 3.25:1 since 1994. A novel decomposition reveals why.
item.author: Dale Impactor III

item.title: IIHS Just Rated 14 Vehicles. The Failures Kill at 5× the Rate of the Winners.
item.description: Cross-referencing July 2026 IIHS test results with a decade of FARS fatality data reveals a 5.2-to-1 death rate gap between vehicles that failed and those that earned Top Safety Pick+.
item.author: Mia Crumplezone

item.title: Your Car Needs Half a Mile of Your Attention to Change the Radio. A 2005 Volvo Needed 328 Feet.
item.description: Vi Bilägare's 2026 repeat study shows touchscreen distraction distances are getting worse, not better. No safety rating on earth penalizes the difference.
item.author: Mia Crumplezone

item.title: NHTSA Called Autonomous Vehicles a "Danger to the General Public." Three Weeks Ago, It Stopped Counting Their Crashes.
item.description: On June 16, NHTSA gutted the only system tracking AV crashes. On July 9, its administrator called AVs dangerous for blocking ambulances. The math doesn't work if you stopped doing the math.
item.author: Clara Rollover

item.title: EVs Have Fewer Moving Parts. One of Ford's Just Broke.
item.description: Ford recalled 42,784 Mustang Mach-Es for a fractured rear differential pinion shaft. BorgWarner supplied it. The fix won't be ready until December.
item.author: Mia Crumplezone

item.title: The Fatality Rate Just Broke Below 1.0. Motorcyclists Are Going the Other Direction.
item.description: Q1 2026 hit 0.99 deaths per 100M VMT — the safest quarter in a decade. Every subcategory declined. Except motorcyclists, who've tripled their death count since 1997.

item.title: Four Recalls, One Failed Crash Test, Six Months: The Hyundai Tucson's 2026
item.description: The 2025-2026 Hyundai Tucson has been recalled four times in six months for four unrelated defects, and earned a POOR whiplash rating from IIHS. It's Hyundai's best-selling vehicle.
item.author: Clara Rollover

item.title: 741,195 Fords Can't Stay in Park. NHTSA Just Proposed Removing Brake Pedals.
item.description: Ford recalled three-quarters of a million vehicles for a rollaway defect the same week NHTSA proposed eliminating brake pedals from robotaxis. The math on defective miles vs. autonomous miles is staggering.
item.author: Rex Driverton

item.title: Stellantis Vehicles Have Killed 23,295 People. The Company Might Not Survive to Fix the Problem.
item.description: Twelve percent of America's traffic deaths belong to Stellantis. So does a €22 billion writedown, a securities fraud lawsuit, and a million Jeeps that catch fire while parked.

item.title: Allstate Just Ranked 200 Cities by Crash Risk. It Missed the Variable That Matters 16x More.
item.description: Allstate's 2026 'Best Drivers' report shows a 4x gap between cities. FARS data shows a 16x gap between popular vehicles at the same dealership. Your car matters more than your zip code.
item.author: Clara Rollover

item.title: Ford Will Spend Millions Telling You It's #1 in Quality. It Recalled 8.9 Cars for Every One It Sold.
item.description: JD Power measures quality for 90 days. The average Ford has been on the road for 4,745 days. Ford's new ad campaign is aimed squarely at the gap between those numbers.
item.author: Axle McScatter

item.title: Congress Mandated a Tire Safety System That Warns You After the Damage Is Done. 600 People Die Every Year Anyway.
item.description: The TREAD Act mandated TPMS after the Firestone scandal killed 271 people. FMVSS 138 set the warning at 25% underinflation. The tire industry now admits that threshold is often too late. Over 600 still die annually.
item.author: Mia Crumplezone

item.title: Three Out of Four Full-Size Pickups Can't Protect a 12-Year-Old in a Crash
item.description: The F-150, Silverado, and RAM 1500 all fail or score poorly on the IIHS rear seat crash test. Only the Tundra passes. Ford hasn't tried to fix it in three years.
item.author: Mia Crumplezone

item.title: GM Has Built the Same Van for Thirty Years and Still Can't Tighten a Nut
item.description: Four recalls in 18 months for the oldest vehicle in production. But the death rate says it's one of the safest vans on the road.
item.author: Mia Crumplezone

item.title: Ford Has Recalled Five Million Vehicles Because They Can't Stay in Park
item.description: Ford's sixth major park-related recall since 2018 pushes the cumulative total past five million vehicles. Three different mechanisms, three drivetrains, one outcome.
item.author: Rex Driverton

item.title: At the Current Rate of Improvement, Vision Zero Arrives in the 23rd Century
item.description: NHTSA pledged zero traffic deaths. We projected the timeline from actual FARS data. At the post-2022 rate of decline, the number drops below one person around the year 2271.

item.title: Your Car Records Everything. Only the Manufacturer Gets to Read It.
item.description: Federal crash recorders capture 15 data points over 5 seconds. Modern ADAS vehicles record thousands of channels continuously. The manufacturer controls who sees them.
item.author: Mia Crumplezone

item.title: A Quarter-Million Land Rovers Have Airbags That Won't Fire. The Fix Is a Dab of Gel.
item.description: 250,857 Defenders, Discoverys, and Range Rovers have a 100% defect rate for driver airbag non-deployment. The cause: normal driving vibrations corrode the clockspring connector. The interim fix: lubricant. No permanent solution exists.
item.author: Vin Wreckage

item.title: Four People Oversee Whether 6 Million Teslas Can Drive Themselves. Congress Wants to Cut Their Budget.
item.description: NHTSA's autonomous vehicle investigation team went from seven people to four after federal workforce cuts. Those four now oversee active safety probes covering 6.1 million Tesla vehicles. Congress proposed cutting the agency's budget by another $88 million.
item.author: Clara Rollover

item.title: NHTSA Closed Two Safety Probes on a Saturday Afternoon. Both Companies Had Already Recalled.
item.description: On June 27, 2026, NHTSA quietly closed investigations into 817,243 Tesla and Honda vehicles. Both manufacturers had already issued voluntary recalls. Tesla said the recall wasn't even in response to the investigation.
item.author: Rex Driverton

item.title: 438 People Will Die This Weekend. Here's When, Where, and in What.
item.description: NSC projects 438 traffic deaths over the July 4th 78-hour window. 38% will involve alcohol — 2.5x the annual baseline. FARS toxicology data reveals which vehicles carry the highest impairment risk.
item.author: Dale Impactor III

item.title: A $20 Chip Would Have Saved Them: The TikTok Theft Epidemic Hiding in FARS Data
item.description: FARS model year data reveals the fingerprint of the Kia Boys epidemic: a 52% death spike for vehicles missing a $20 immobilizer, and an 82% drop the year it was finally added.
item.author: Rex Driverton

item.title: Every Car in America Has the Same Backdoor That Killed Martha Avila
item.description: Tesla says the driver overrode Full Self-Driving at 73 mph. But the override isn't a Tesla problem. It's an industry design choice built into every AEB-equipped vehicle on the road.
item.author: Clara Rollover

item.title: The U.S. Has Two Official Counts of Its Road Dead. They're 3,535 Bodies Apart.
item.description: NHTSA says 39,254 died on American roads in 2024. The CDC says 42,789. Both numbers are correct. The gap reveals who counts and who doesn't.
item.author: Vin Wreckage

item.title: America Has Had Three Great Death Declines. The First Two Ended Badly.
item.description: NHTSA data reveals the current 15-quarter decline in traffic fatalities is the third-longest streak in FARS history. The previous two reversed violently when recessions ended. This one might be structurally different: it's driven by fleet turnover, not economics.
item.author: Dale Impactor III

item.title: Land Rover's Fix for 250,857 Potentially Non-Deploying Airbags Is a Dab of Grease. It Was a Dab of Grease in 2016, Too.
item.description: JLR recalled a quarter-million SUVs because corroding clockspring connectors might prevent airbag deployment. The fix is lubricant gel. A 2016 NHTSA bulletin shows JLR knew the same connectors corroded a decade ago. The connector was never redesigned.
item.author: Clara Rollover

item.title: NHTSA Mandated a Safety System That Prevents 360 Deaths a Year. It Also Causes the Exact Crash It Was Designed to Stop.
item.description: Hyundai recalled 421,078 vehicles for phantom braking. Honda has 3 million under investigation. NHTSA's AEB mandate includes two false-positive tests the agency admits are "by no means comprehensive."

item.title: NHTSA Investigates 100 Crashes a Year. In 2026, It's Investigated 2.
item.description: NHTSA's Special Crash Investigations program has opened just 2 cases in 2026, a 96% collapse coinciding with DOGE staffing cuts that gutted the agency's autonomous vehicle safety division.

item.title: Every State Banned Texting While Driving. 32,717 People Died Anyway.
item.description: Cellphone ban effectiveness decays after two years. In 19 states, texting-only bans may actually increase fatal crashes. A decade of distraction laws, 32,717 deaths, and the annual toll hasn't moved.

item.title: Ford Filed 51 Recalls This Year. That Might Make Them the Safest Manufacturer in America.
item.description: Ford leads all automakers in recalls because a consent order forces disclosure. FARS data shows the company with fewer recalls kills more drivers per mile.

item.title: IIHS Wants to Cut Crash Deaths 30% by 2030. They Need 5.8% Annual Declines. They're Getting 3.8%.
item.description: The 30x30 vision requires historically unprecedented sustained declines. The vehicle categories killing the most Americans can't pass IIHS's own tests.

item.title: Stellantis Killed the Hybrid to Stop the Fires. The Wrangler Kept Burning.
item.description: Four fire recalls in three years across two unrelated ignition pathways. Stellantis killed its entire plug-in hybrid line. Then a million non-hybrid Wranglers needed the same warning: park outside.

item.title: Every Sedan Driver Drinks the Same. Only Some of Them Die.
item.description: Impairment rates are statistically identical across 22 popular sedans. Death rates span 100x. The car is the variable.

item.title: America Got Safer in 2024. Unless You're Over 65.
item.description: Traffic deaths dropped below 40,000 for the first time since 2020. But fatalities among Americans 65 and older climbed 2%, making seniors the deadliest age group on the road.

item.title: Toyota Built the Safest SUV in America. Its EVs Can't Light a Curve.
item.description: The RAV4 posts the lowest fatality rate of any SUV in the FARS database. But every Toyota EV on sale today earned a Poor headlight rating from IIHS, disqualifying all three from safety awards.

item.title: Honda Built Its V6 for 27 Years. Then 1.4 Million of Them Started Eating Their Own Bearings.
item.description: Honda's legendary J35 V6 has a crankshaft manufacturing defect affecting 1.4 million of America's safest family vehicles. NHTSA expanded its investigation after Honda denied every post-recall complaint.

item.title: Nobody Crash-Tests America's Work Trucks. That's 6,535 Funerals a Year.
item.description: Heavy-duty trucks and cargo vans are exempt from federal crash-test standards. The IIHS just tested them anyway. Five of nine failed.

item.title: Ford Recalled Your Car, Fixed It, Filed the Paperwork. The Fix Was Fake.
item.description: Ford's dealer diagnostic tool silently reported failed software updates as complete. 318,367 vehicles are being re-recalled. The affected Focus fleet has 1,042 FARS deaths.

item.title: America Just Spent Six Years and 24,000 Lives Getting Back to Square One
item.description: NHTSA's 2025 estimate puts the fatality rate back at 2019 levels. The roundtrip cost approximately 23,747 excess deaths. No peer country experienced anything comparable.

item.title: Honda Built the Safest SUVs in America. Then Forgot to Rustproof Them.
item.description: 880,514 Honda Pilots, Passports, Ridgelines, and Acura MDXs recalled for rear subframe corrosion. FARS data shows these are among the safest vehicles on the road. Honda knew about the paint defect for 4.5 years.

item.title: 31 States Banned Your Phone. The Wrong Car Kills 12 Times as Many People.
item.description: Pennsylvania just started fining drivers $50 for holding their phones. FARS data shows vehicle choice determines survival 12 to 37 times more than hands-free laws ever could.

item.title: The 'Ultimate Driving Machine' Is the Ultimate Luxury Sedan Killer
item.description: BMW 3 Series drivers die at 4.3x the rate of Mercedes C-Class drivers. Impairment rates are identical. FARS data reveals the deadliest luxury sedan in America.

item.title: 36,640 Dead. The Decline Is Real. Half of It Is Just Old Cars Dying.
item.description: Traffic deaths dropped 6.7% in 2025. We decomposed the decline using FARS fleet data. Roughly half the improvement comes from pre-ESC vehicles scrapping out of existence.

item.title: 2,028 People Killed by "Legal" Drivers Last Year. One State Had the Fix Since 2018.
item.description: In 2024, 2,028 people died in crashes where a driver's BAC was between .01 and .07 — below the legal limit in 49 states. Utah dropped to .05 in 2018 and cut fatal crashes 19.8%. The NTSB recommended the same thing nationally in 2013. Nobody listened.

item.title: Subaru Built the Safest Cars in America. In 2026, Half a Million of Them Got Recalled.
item.description: Subaru's 0.43 deaths per 100M VMT makes it the safest major brand in FARS data. Then 530,000 vehicles got recalled for parts falling off, leaking fuel, and corroding brackets.

item.title: Toyota Built 270,000 Engines That Could Seize at Highway Speed. Then It Fixed the Problem. Then the Fix Failed.
item.description: Three recall waves, one defect. Toyota's V35A twin-turbo V6 replaced a V8 that made the Tundra the second-safest full-size pickup. Now it's a $2.7 billion manufacturing failure.

item.title: Ford Used the Nuclear Option on a Ball Joint. That Warning Used to Mean Your Car Contains a Bomb.
item.description: NHTSA's 'do not drive' order was built for Takata airbags that could kill you with shrapnel. Ford just deployed it for a stud that wasn't pushed in far enough at a factory in Mexico.

item.title: Five States Saved 250 Pedestrians. Twenty-Four States Killed More.
item.description: The 'largest pedestrian safety improvement in 15 years' is driven by five states. Twenty-four states got worse. The national number is a mirage.

item.title: 202,345 Truck Drivers Are Banned From Driving. Nobody Took Their Keys.
item.description: The FMCSA Clearinghouse lists 202,345 prohibited CDL holders. 159,226 never started rehabilitation. FARS data shows 24% of truck drivers in fatal crashes had no valid CDL.

item.title: 1.5 Million Vehicles Recalled Because Their Dashboards Forgot How to Be Dashboards
item.description: Seven manufacturers, five different suppliers, eighteen months, 1.5 million vehicles recalled for the same defect: digital instrument clusters that go blank while driving. The analog gauge never had this problem.

item.title: Tesla Claims FSD Is 10x Safer Than You. Three Statistical Distortions Say Otherwise.
item.description: Reuters found 10 of 11 traffic-safety researchers call Tesla's FSD safety stats misleading. The math is even worse than they reported. Correct for all three distortions and the advantage collapses to ~1.1x.

item.title: Hyundai Built a Safety System That Causes Crashes. It Knew for 16 Months. 421,078 Vehicles Just Got the Recall.
item.description: The Forward Collision Avoidance system slammed the brakes on empty roads, turning 421K Tucsons into rear-end hazards. Hyundai investigated in January 2025. The recall came in May 2026.

item.title: Three Recalls, Three Failure Modes, 527,652 Vehicles. The Airbag Was Supposed to Be the Easy Part.
item.description: Honda's bags fire at children. Stellantis's fire too late. GM's fire as shrapnel. All in the same week. FARS says these models account for 18,878 deaths.

item.title: 850 People Died in Work Zones Last Year. Four Out of Five Weren't Wearing Orange.
item.description: FHWA says 850 died in work zone crashes in 2024. Roughly 80% were motorists, not construction workers. The I-81 bus crash that killed 5 happened in one.

item.title: America's Two Deadliest Trucks Can't Pass a Crash Test. The Meme Truck Aced It.
item.description: The F-150 and Silverado have 18,785 combined deaths. Neither earned an IIHS safety award in 2026. The Cybertruck earned the highest one.

item.title: Lucid Has Recalled 2,039 Cars for Losing Power Without Warning. This Is the Third Time.
item.description: Three recalls, three different failure mechanisms, one outcome: 4,500-pound sedans losing all drive power at highway speed. The inverter keeps finding new ways to quit.

item.title: 30 Years Ago, Airbags Killed Children. The Fix Was One Sensor. Honda Just Recalled 99,000 of Them.
item.description: The passenger seat weight sensor was the entire regulatory response to a child-death crisis. In 98,892 Hondas, it cracks and short-circuits, telling the airbag every passenger is an adult.

item.title: The Safest Midsize Truck on American Roads Just Got the Worst Crash Test Score
item.description: Honda's Ridgeline has a 0.24 fatality rate per 100M VMT — lowest of any midsize pickup by 12x. Then IIHS gave it three Poor ratings in May 2026. Both are correct.

item.title: NHTSA Says 3,208 People Died From Distracted Driving. The Real Number Is Closer to 10,000.
item.description: NHTSA counts 3,208 distracted driving deaths. The NSC estimates 24% of all crashes involve phones. The gap exists because we never built a breathalyzer for inattention.

item.title: A $100 Device Prevents 67% of Repeat Drunk Driving. 80% of Offenders Never Install It.
item.description: Ignition interlocks cut alcohol-impaired fatal crashes by 26% where enforced. The GAO found only 15-20% of convicted DUI offenders actually install one. The compliance gap costs roughly 2,400 lives per year.

item.title: NHTSA Can Force Recalls. After 997 in 2025, It Used That Power Exactly Once.
item.description: NHTSA issued its first mandatory recall order in decades for 12 aftermarket Chinese airbag inflators. Meanwhile, ARC Automotive's 52 million domestic inflators remain unrecalled after 32 months of procedural delay.

item.title: NHTSA Used Its Most Powerful Enforcement Tool for the First Time in 20 Years. It Can't Find the Cars.
item.description: The agency issued its first compulsory recall order in two decades against counterfeit Chinese airbag inflators that killed 10 people. The manufacturer is in China. The parts were imported illegally. No list of affected vehicles exists. The recall is a press release.

item.title: The Agency Protecting 280 Million Drivers Spends $0.13 Per Vehicle Per Year Finding Defects. Congress Wants to Spend Less.
item.description: NHTSA's defect investigation office misses deadlines on 94% of its probes. The average investigation takes 617 days, more than five times its own target. Congress is cutting the budget anyway.

item.title: FARS Changed How It Counts Drugs in 2018. The 'Epidemic' Didn't Spike. The Spreadsheet Did.
item.description: Columbia University analyzed 138,505 dead drivers and found the post-2018 drugged driving surge is largely a measurement artifact. FARS expanded its drug fields and the numbers jumped 42%. Alcohol, counted correctly all along, barely moved.

item.title: We Called the Ridgeline the Safest Pickup in America. Then IIHS Crashed One.
item.description: The Honda Ridgeline has the lowest FARS death rate of any midsize pickup at 0.24 per 100M VMT. On May 19, IIHS gave it a Poor rating. Both are true. Neither is lying.

item.title: NHTSA's Newest Mandate Will Save 50 Lives a Year. That's a Rounding Error.
item.description: NHTSA mandated rear-seat seatbelt alarms for 2027 vehicles. Their own cost-benefit analysis says it will save 50 lives per year out of 9,758 unbuckled deaths. That is 0.5 percent of the problem.

item.title: America's Traffic Death Rate Just Hit a Record Low. It's the Same Number From 2014.
item.description: The 2025 fatality rate of 1.10 per 100M VMT is being celebrated as historic progress. In 2014, it was 1.08. A decade of safety mandates bought the country two hundredths of a point.

item.title: NHTSA Used Its Recall Power for the First Time in 20 Years. The Target Couldn't Fight Back.
item.description: NHTSA's first forced recall in decades targeted a Chinese airbag inflator maker with no US lawyers, no lobbyists, and no ability to resist. GM and Takata killed hundreds and never faced the same authority.

item.title: Ford BlueCruise Killed 3 People and Recorded Zero Data Points
item.description: The NTSB found Ford's hands-free driving system can't see stopped cars at highway speed. When it crashes, it records nothing. The regulatory gap covers 2.5 million vehicles.

item.title: 43% of Car Ads Sell Speed. 3% Mention Safety. 11,288 People Died.
item.description: IIHS found automakers tripled speed advertising while cutting safety messaging by 73 percent. The death toll didn't budge.

item.title: For 48 Years, NHTSA Rated Cars on How Well They Crumpled. One Car Finally Passed the Other Test.
item.description: NHTSA spent 48 years rating cars on crumple zones. In 2026, they finally tested whether cars try not to crash. One car passed.

item.title: 47% of Q1 Recalls Were Electrical. The System Was Built for Brake Lines.
item.description: 12.1 million vehicles recalled in Q1 2026. Nearly half for electrical defects. Half fixable by wireless patch. The verification framework hasn't changed since 1966.

item.title: NYC Put Speed Governors on 700 Cars; Speeding Dropped 64%; Nobody Sells Them
item.description: A $200 device cut speeding by 64% in NYC fleet vehicles. NHTSA says speed kills 12,000 per year. 64% of 12,000 is 7,600. No US automaker installs it.

item.title: 1 in 5 Impaired Drivers Who Die in Crashes Are on Alcohol AND Drugs. The Breathalyzer Catches Half.
item.description: FARS toxicology data: 18,336 dead drivers tested positive for both alcohol and drugs. Polysubstance impairment grew 38% in four years. America has 10,000 cops trained to detect it.

item.title: The U.S. Death Rate Hit 1.10. For 23 Million Vehicles, It’s Still 2.75.
item.description: Cross-tabulating FARS per-model fatality rates against fleet share reveals that America's 1.10 death rate is a weighted average of three statistically distinct vehicle populations, with a 6.9x gap between them.

item.title: Ford Issued 153 Recalls Last Year. 98% of Their Death Toll Can’t Receive One.
item.description: FARS data reveals that 98.4% of Ford's fatal crash deaths involve pre-2020 vehicles unreachable by OTA software fixes, even as Ford touts 80% of its 2026 recalls as over-the-air updates.

item.title: The Junkyard Dividend: America’s ‘Safest Year’ on the Road Is Really About Scrapping Death Traps
item.description: NHTSA says 2025 was the safest year since 2019. FARS data says the real hero is the scrapyard: 65% of fatal-crash vehicles are pre-2010 models now rusting into oblivion.

item.title: 63 Vehicles Won IIHS Awards. America’s Best-Selling Trucks Weren’t Among Them.
item.description: Cross-referencing 2026 IIHS awards against a decade of FARS data reveals a 4.7x death rate gap between award winners and the trucks Americans actually buy. Zero minivans qualified.

item.title: Traffic Deaths Dropped 6.7%. Thank the Scrapyard, Not the Senate.
item.description: 62.3% of all FARS fatalities involve vehicles from 2008 or earlier. As pre-ESC cars get scrapped, deaths fall mechanically. The 2025 decline is about physics and rust, not policy.

item.title: Congress Is Fighting a ‘Kill Switch’ That Doesn’t Exist. The 44,000 Dead Since 2021 Are Real.
item.description: The HALT Act mandated alcohol detection tech in all new cars. NHTSA missed its deadline. Congress wants to defund it. 44,000 people died in alcohol-impaired crashes while everyone argued about a technology that doesn't exist yet.

item.title: Your Car’s Emergency Braking Was Tested at Parking Lot Speeds. It Fails at Highway Speeds.
item.description: IIHS certifies AEB at 12-25 mph. Only 3% of rear-end crashes happen that slowly. AAA tested 2024 models at 55 mph. None stopped in time. The federal rule that would fix this is frozen.

item.title: The Pandemic Is Over. The Pandemic Driving Isn’t.
item.description: NHTSA celebrated 39,345 deaths as progress. At pre-COVID rates, 2,295 fewer people would have died. Five years of behavioral drift have killed roughly 27,600 Americans nobody is counting.

item.title: Your Airbag Might Be a Grenade. NHTSA Found 10 Dead Drivers to Prove It.
item.description: Counterfeit Chinese airbag inflators have killed 10 drivers in survivable crashes. FARS data reveals why the Malibu and Sonata were targeted.

item.title: America’s Biggest Safety Initiative Just Targeted the Wrong Variable
item.description: NHTSA's Pathways to Safer Streets focuses on driver behavior, but FARS data shows impairment holds constant at ~20% across all vehicle models while death rates vary 284-fold. The initiative addresses the constant and ignores the variable.

item.title: America’s Death Rate Is 1.10. The Cars Say It Should Be 0.75.
item.description: The 2025 fleet is radically safer than 2014's. The death rate barely budged. Something is consuming the engineering dividend, and it fits in your pocket.

item.title: Opioid Deaths Dropped 26%. Traffic Deaths Dropped 6.7%. Nobody Connected Them.
item.description: CDC reports the largest overdose decline ever recorded. NHTSA reports the lowest traffic death rate in decades. FARS toxicology data suggests the two trends share a common variable nobody is talking about.

item.title: The Dodge Grand Caravan Killed 1,782 Americans. It Was Supposed to Be the Safe Choice.
item.description: FARS data reveals a 7x death rate gap within the minivan category. The Grand Caravan posted 1.33 deaths per 100M VMT. Its replacement, the Chrysler Pacifica, posts 0.19.

item.title: Kia Is Statistically the Safest Brand in America. Nobody Noticed.
item.description: FARS data across a decade shows Kia's brand-average fatality percentile is 11.2%. Toyota's is 48.1%. The budget brand is the safety brand.

item.title: 31 Cars Almost Never Kill Their Occupants. You Can Buy 28 of Them Today.
item.description: A cluster of 31 vehicles in the FARS database have near-zero occupant death rates. The engineering is solved. The information gap isn't.

item.title: BMW's Safest Car Beats Every Volvo. BMW's Deadliest Is 30 Times Worse.
item.description: FARS data shows Volvo's death rate spread across all models is just 2.6x. BMW's is 30x. The safest brand isn't about having the best car. It's about never selling a dangerous one.

item.title: 225,000 Stellantis Vehicles Were Already Killing People. Now They Have Bombs in the Steering Wheel.
item.description: Stellantis says don't drive these cars. FARS says 4,071 people in those exact model years are already dead. The airbag fix is free. 225,000 owners haven't done it.

item.title: America's DUI Body Count Belongs to the Walmart Parking Lot
item.description: The top five DUI-fatality vehicles in FARS are the Silverado, F-150, Accord, Camry, and Civic. Five appliances producing 4.6 impaired fatal crashes per day. None has an impairment rate above the class average.

item.title: The $14,730 Question: How Much Crash Survival Can You Actually Buy?
item.description: The Nissan Versa was America's cheapest new car. FARS data shows its occupants die 72.3% of the time in fatal crashes. Budget subcompacts as a class run 71-75% lethality vs 39-56% for midsize vehicles.

item.title: The Mini Cooper Has Go-Kart Handling. It Also Has Go-Kart Crumple Zones.
item.description: FARS data shows the Mini Cooper has a per-crash lethality of 0.653, higher than every crossover SUV in the database. Its drivers are among the soberest in fatal crashes. The problem is 2,700 pounds of physics.

item.title: Same Brand, Same Price, 10x the Death Rate
item.description: FARS data exposes a brutal gap between sedans and crossovers from the same manufacturer. The Toyota Camry's death rate is 968% higher than the RAV4's. Every sedan lost.

item.title: Hyundai Wins Every Safety Award. FARS Says It Barely Matters.
item.description: A brand-level FARS analysis reveals Hyundai cut model-year fatalities just 6% while Ford and Chevy dropped 76%. Awards and actual deaths tell different stories.

item.title: 1.9 Million Vehicles Just Lost the Safety Feature That Saves 78% of Backover Victims
item.description: Ford and Toyota recalled nearly 1.9 million SUVs with defective rearview cameras in March 2026. NHTSA data shows only 25% of recalled vehicles ever get fixed, leaving an estimated 1.4 million drivers blind in reverse.

item.title: Your Car's Death Rate Is Hiding Two Completely Different Failures
item.description: Some vehicles crash constantly but protect you. Others almost never crash — but when they do, you die. FARS data reveals a two-axis safety map that the single death-rate number was designed to obscure.

channel.description: Every fatal crash in America, charted. NHTSA FARS data investigations.

item.title: Ford Issued 153 Recalls in One Year. None of Them Fixed What Actually Kills People.
item.description: Ford set the all-time recall record in 2025 with 153 safety recalls. Cross-referencing those recalls with FARS fatality data reveals zero overlap between what Ford is fixing and what is killing Ford drivers.

item.title: At 4,000 Pounds, Your Vehicle Stops Getting Safer. It Just Gets Deadlier.
item.description: A 2025 IIHS study found that vehicle weight stops protecting drivers above 4,000 lbs. FARS data confirms: the Ram 2500 survives 80% of its fatal crashes. The person in the other car doesn't.

item.title: One Redesign Erased 84% of a Car's Deaths. Most Recalls Don't Come Close.
item.description: The 2020 Sentra redesign cut model-year deaths from 173 to 30. FARS data shows platform redesigns routinely save more lives than defect recalls.

item.title: America's Safest Minivan Just Got Recalled for the One Thing Keeping It Safe
item.description: The Chrysler Pacifica is 7x safer than the Grand Caravan it replaced. Now 178,000 of them have defective side curtain airbags. FARS data quantifies the stakes.

item.title: Same Price, Different Coffin: The Death Rate Lottery Hiding in Your Car Payment
item.description: FARS data reveals 4-8x death rate spreads between vehicles at the same price point. Your sticker price tells you nothing about your chances of survival.

item.title: A $22,000 Kia Sedan Protects Your Kids Better Than Any Minivan on Sale
item.description: Zero out of four minivans earned an IIHS safety award in 2026. The Honda Odyssey scored Poor for rear-seat protection. A Kia K4 starting at $22,290 got Top Safety Pick+. Your family hauler is a front-seat safety pod.

item.title: Your Garage Door Is Better Regulated Than Your Car Seat
item.description: A 2-year-old was crushed by a Hyundai Palisade power seat. Power windows killed children for 35 years before automakers added an $8 fix. Your garage door has had mandatory anti-crush protection since 1993. Your car seat still doesn't.

item.title: FSD Is Trying to Replace the Safest Drivers in America
item.description: NHTSA is investigating 3.2 million Teslas for FSD failures. FARS data shows Tesla drivers already have the lowest crash rate of any vehicle in the dataset. The paradox: FSD may be making America's safest fleet less safe.

item.title: 39,345 Dead. NHTSA Calls It Progress.
item.description: NHTSA's 2024 preliminary data shows traffic fatalities dropped below 40,000 for the first time since 2020. Our FARS decomposition reveals 72% of deaths come from pre-2012 vehicles simply aging off the road. Progress by attrition.

item.title: One Corporation, 51,687 Deaths: GM's Multi-Brand Body Count
item.description: Chevrolet, GMC, Buick, Pontiac, Cadillac, Saturn, Oldsmobile. Seven brands, one parent company, 27% of all FARS fatalities. Brand-level data splits the deaths across seven ledgers. Stack them and the concentration is staggering.

item.title: Every Car Has the Same Drunk Drivers — So Why Do Death Rates Vary 285x?
item.description: Impairment rates hold steady at ~20% across 200 vehicle models. Death rates swing from 0.03 to 8.54 per 100M VMT. R² = 0.038. The vehicle is the variable, not the driver.

item.title: The Convertible Rollover Gap: Which Open-Top Cars Actually Protect You, and Which Ones Just Look Like They Do
item.description: Fixed steel hoops vs. pyrotechnic pop-up bars vs. reinforced A-pillars. We compared rollover protection across nine popular convertibles. Track day organizations already know the answer.

item.title: Your Car Was Designed to Save You in a Rollover. Not to See the Person in the Crosswalk.
item.description: IIHS measured blind zones in 168 vehicles. A large A-pillar blind zone raises left-turn pedestrian crash risk by 70%. The pillars that save occupants in rollovers are hiding pedestrians from drivers.

item.title: 919,000 Crashes. 2,872 Dead. Nobody Stayed.
item.description: Hit-and-run crashes hit a record 15% of all police-reported crashes in 2023. One in four pedestrians killed was left on the road. The poorest ZIP codes absorb six times the deaths.

item.title: Saturn's Dent-Proof Panels Couldn't Stop It From Being the Deadliest Car Per Crash in America
item.description: 92.4% lethality rate — worst in the FARS database. The polymer body panels that survived shopping carts couldn't save anyone at highway speed.

item.title: The Car With America's Drunkest Drivers Is a Buick
item.description: The Buick Park Avenue leads every vehicle in the FARS database for impaired-driver fatal crashes at 31.7%. It beats the Corvette, the Mustang, and the Camaro.

item.title: Nissan Called the Maxima a "4-Door Sports Car." The Fatality Data Agrees.
item.description: At 5.11 deaths per 100M VMT, the Maxima is deadlier than the Camaro, the Corvette, and the Challenger. Nissan's marketing was more accurate than they intended.

item.title: The F-150 Was Involved in 20,066 Fatal Crashes. In 10,872 of Them, Someone Else Died.
item.description: When pickups crash, more than half the fatalities are outside the truck. The Ram's 5,703 external kills would rank between drowning and fire as a cause of death. Nobody tracks this metric.

item.title: 86% of Cavalier Crashes Are Fatal. For a Ram 2500, It's 21%.
item.description: Death rates tell you how often a vehicle crashes. Lethality ratios tell you what happens next. In 337 models of FARS data, the survivability gap is 4 to 1 — and it tracks perfectly with weight.

item.title: Mississippi Kills Drivers at 5× the Rate of Massachusetts. They Drive the Same Cars.
item.description: 24.9 deaths per 100K in Mississippi. 4.9 in Massachusetts. Same Camrys, same F-150s. The gap isn't the vehicles — it's the roads, the speeds, and the distance to a trauma center.

item.title: Congress Saved 9,000 Lives a Year in 2007. The Cars Didn't Get the Memo Until 2024.
item.description: The ESC mandate passed in 2007. Full fleet penetration took 17 years. In the gap: roughly 40,000 people died in vehicles that would have had stability control if the fleet turned over faster.

item.title: A Porsche 911 Driver Drinks as Much as a Cobalt Driver. One Is 7× More Likely to Survive.
item.description: Impairment rates are nearly identical across price points — 22.8% vs 22.4%. Death rates differ by up to 26.5×. Safety in America is a luxury good, and the data proves it.

item.title: 80% of Fatal Crash Drivers Were Sober. America Spent $800 Million on the Other 20%.
item.description: 490,736 drivers in fatal crashes. 80% had zero impairment. NHTSA spends $800M/year chasing the minority. The Toyota Solara's 4.1% impairment rate is the lowest in FARS. Its death rate is 3× the Camry's.

item.title: Seven Vehicles That Got 8–22× Safer in a Single Generation
item.description: The Tracker killed at 7.83. Its Equinox replacement does 0.36. That's a 22-fold improvement. Seven vehicles, seven engineering events, and the three changes that explain all of them.

item.title: Sedans Kill at 2.5× the Rate of SUVs. Every Single Brand.
item.description: 89,127 sedan deaths vs 46,442 SUV deaths. Across every major automaker, the sedan is deadlier per mile than the SUV. The gaps range from 2× to 25×. No exceptions.

item.title: GM Built One Minivan and Sold It Four Times. All Four Were Deadly.
item.description: 715 deaths across the Venture, Montana, Uplander, and Silhouette — four badges on one bad platform. The Sienna was 3.6× safer per mile. GM's exit from minivans was a retreat, not a pivot.

item.title: Half the People Dying on American Roads Are Dying in Cars That Don't Exist Anymore
item.description: 46.7% of all FARS fatalities came from vehicles built before 2006. Nine discontinued models have 100% of their deaths from the ghost fleet. 87,320 people killed in cars no manufacturer will defend.

item.title: The Chrysler Sebring Had the Lowest Impairment Rate in Its Class. It Killed 664 People Anyway.
item.description: 17.6% impairment — lowest of any midsize sedan in FARS. Yet at 1.65 deaths per 100M VMT, sober people were dying in a car that folded on impact. Then Chrysler fixed it, got worse drivers, and the death rate dropped anyway.

item.title: Honda's Sedans Are Deadly. Honda's SUVs Are Among the Safest Cars in America.
item.description: The Accord kills at 3.07 per 100M VMT. The Pilot kills at 0.29. Same brand, same dealership, 10.6× safety gap. Honda's two best-selling sedans have killed 13,655 people.

item.title: The Dodge Neon Was America's Disposable First Car. It Killed 602 People.
item.description: 23.2% impairment including 11.8% drug-positive — nearly double the national average. Model year 2005 alone: 159 deaths. The car nobody chose on purpose became a toxicology case study.

item.title: Every Subaru Is Safer Than Its Competitors. Every Single One.
item.description: Crosstrek at 0.08 is 12× safer than the Ford Escape. WRX at 0.29 is 21× safer than the Mustang — despite higher impairment. Seven models, seven class wins. The brand nobody recommends for safety is the safest brand in the database.

item.title: The Toyota Prius Is the Safest Sedan in America. Its Drivers Are the Soberest, Too.
item.description: The Prius kills at 0.55 per 100M VMT — 3.4× safer than the Corolla, 5.6× safer than the Accord. 16% impairment rate — lowest of any major sedan. The car everyone mocks is the one that's least likely to kill you.

item.title: The Dodge Challenger Is the Safest Muscle Car in America. Nobody Believes It.
item.description: The Challenger kills at 1.00 per 100M VMT — 6× safer than the Mustang, 3.4× safer than the Camaro. Same impairment rates across the segment. It's the heaviest, the oldest-skewing, and the one nobody thought to check.

item.title: The Cadillac Seville Had the Lowest Impairment Rate in the Database. It Killed 391 People Anyway.
item.description: Cadillac's premium sedan has the highest death rate of any luxury car in FARS — 3.89 per 100M VMT. Its drivers were the soberest in the database at 10.5% impairment. The DeVille, one rung down the ladder, is 10× safer.

item.title: The Ford E-350 Killed 776 People. Most Were Passengers.
item.description: America's default church bus, airport shuttle, and hotel van has the highest death rate of any commercial vehicle — 2.51 per 100M VMT. Its replacement, the Transit, kills at 0.14. An 18-fold safety gap Ford took 54 years to close.

item.title: America's Cheapest Cars Are a Death Lottery. The Odds Depend on the Badge.
item.description: The Chevy Sonic kills at 1.40 per 100M VMT. The Mitsubishi Mirage manages 0.40. A 3.5× safety gap in the segment where buyers can least afford to get it wrong — and GM's sober Spark drivers are dying at higher rates than Hyundai's drunk Accent drivers.

item.title: The Toyota 4Runner Is 5× Deadlier Than the RAV4. Guess Which One Toyota Calls "Rugged."
item.description: 1,418 deaths and a 1.00 rate per 100M VMT make the 4Runner Toyota's most dangerous SUV — 5× deadlier per mile than the RAV4, 2.4× the Highlander. Same company, similar buyers, radically different body counts.

item.title: The Chevy Silverado Is the Deadliest Vehicle in America. The F-150 Is Catching Up.
item.description: 9,591 deaths — more than any other vehicle in FARS. Add the Sierra badge twin and GM's full-size truck platform has killed 12,928 people. The F-150 is 20% safer per mile. The Ram is 37% safer.

item.title: The Volkswagen Jetta Promised German Engineering. It Delivered 1,375 Deaths.
item.description: VW's own Golf on the same platform scores 0.18 deaths per 100M VMT. The Jetta? 1.71. That's 9.5× deadlier — same company, same architecture, radically different outcomes.

item.title: Minivan Drivers Are the Soberest People on the Road. And It's Not Even Close.
item.description: Minivans average 15.4% impairment in fatal crashes vs. 20% nationally. Sports cars hit 22.5%. The Grand Caravan and Odyssey clock in at 15.3% and 15.4%. The car you drive is a behavioral confession.

item.title: GM Sold the Same Deadly SUV Twice. The Yukon and Tahoe Have Killed 3,706 People.
item.description: Same platform, same factory, same death rate. The Yukon (2.55) and Tahoe (2.49) are mechanically identical — and 3× deadlier per mile than the Toyota Sequoia. Add the Suburban and it's 4,299 deaths from one architecture.

item.title: Hyundai and Kia Build the Same Car. The Hyundai Kills 3.8× More People.
item.description: Same platform, same engines, same parent company. The Elantra kills at 1.50 per 100M VMT — the Forte at 0.40. The Sonata-Optima gap is 2.7×. Korean twins, wildly different body counts.

item.title: The Ford Fusion Was the Safest Midsize Sedan in America. Ford Killed It Anyway.
item.description: At 1.23 deaths per 100M VMT, the Fusion was 40% safer than the Camry and 60% safer than the Accord. Same impairment rates across the segment. Ford discontinued it in 2020.

item.title: One Designer Made the Same Deadly Car Twice. For Two Different Companies.
item.description: Bryan Nesbitt designed the PT Cruiser at Chrysler, then the HHR at GM. Same retro concept, 1,187 combined deaths. The HHR kills at 2.12 per 100M VMT — 63% deadlier per mile.

item.title: Nissan Sold the Same Truck for 16 Years. It Killed 1,030 People.
item.description: The Frontier went virtually unchanged from 2004 to 2021 while competitors redesigned around it. Rate: 1.45 per 100M VMT — 1.8× the Tacoma, 5.2× the Colorado.

item.title: The Ford Escape Is 5× Deadlier Than a RAV4. They Cost the Same.
item.description: The compact SUV segment hides a 5× safety gap: the Escape kills at 0.95 per 100M VMT while the RAV4 manages 0.19. Same price, same cross-shopping — wildly different odds.

item.title: Infiniti Built Three Generations of the Same Car. The Impairment Rate Never Changed.
item.description: The G35, G37, and Q50 span 20 years and 698 deaths. Across all three, ~24% of fatal-crash drivers were impaired — 4 points above the national average, every single generation.

item.title: The Chrysler 300 Kills 2.5× More People Than the Dodge Charger. They're the Same Car.
item.description: Same LX platform, same factory, same engines. The "respectable" sedan kills at 1.87 per 100M VMT — the muscle car at 0.75.

item.title: Toyota Built a Camry Coupe. It's 2× Deadlier Than the Camry.
item.description: The Solara shares its platform, engine, and factory with the Camry. It kills at 4.25 per 100M VMT — more than double the Camry's 2.03. Only 4.1% impairment. Sober people dying in a Toyota.

item.title: Pontiac Died in 2010. It Has Killed 3,038 People Since.
item.description: GM killed the brand, but 2.45 million Pontiacs still haunt American roads — Grand Prix (970 deaths), G6 (908), Grand Am (713). Every single FARS death came from a ghost.

item.title: The Dodge Dakota Was 3× Deadlier Than a Tacoma. Dodge Just Killed It.
item.description: At 2.62 deaths per 100M VMT from a 350K fleet, the Dakota was the second-deadliest mid-size pickup — and Dodge's answer was to discontinue it entirely.

item.title: The Toyota Corolla Has Killed 4,945 People. It's Still the Safest Car on This List.
item.description: The world's best-selling car is the 4th deadliest sedan in FARS — not because it's dangerous, but because 2.3 million of them drive 26.7 billion miles a year.

item.title: The Chevy Tracker Was a Suzuki in Disguise. It Killed 856 People.
item.description: At 7.83 deaths per 100M VMT, the rebadged Suzuki Vitara was 41× deadlier per mile than the RAV4 it competed against — and GM put a bowtie on it.

item.title: The Chevy S-10 Was 6× Deadlier Than a Tacoma. Then GM Fixed It.
item.description: At 4.83 deaths per 100M VMT, the S-10 was the deadliest compact pickup by a grotesque margin — then the Colorado replacement dropped it to 0.28.

item.title: The Ford Crown Victoria Killed 881 People. Most of Them Were on the Clock.
item.description: America's most trusted fleet vehicle — chosen by police, taxis, and government agencies — had a fuel tank that turned rear-end crashes into cremations.

item.title: The Ford Expedition: America's Most Trusted Family Coffin
The Chevy Cavalier Killed 1,225 People. Then GM Made Its Replacement Worse.
Grandma's Buick LeSabre Has a Higher Death Rate Than the Toyota Camry
item.description: 1,344 dead at 2.67 per 100M VMT — 32% deadlier than the Camry, with 23.5% impairment. The retirement sedan was secretly lethal.

item.title: The Jeep Wrangler Is America's Favorite Way to Die Outdoors
GM Built the Same Deadly SUV Twice. The Trailblazer Killed 2,473 People.
The Chevy Malibu Has Killed 3,465 People. Nobody Noticed.
The Chevy Tahoe Weighs 5,600 Pounds. It Didn't Help.
Mercury Died in 2011. The Grand Marquis Is Still Killing People.
The Jeep Cherokee Is 3.4× Deadlier Than the Grand Cherokee. The Name Is a Trap.
The Ford Ranger Is Nearly 3× Deadlier Than the F-150. Small Trucks Are a Lie.
The Nissan Sentra Is the Cheapest Way to Die in a Sedan
The BMW 3 Series Is the Deadliest Luxury Car in America. By a Lot.
The Dodge Grand Caravan Killed 1,782 People. Almost None of Them Were Drunk.
The Ford Taurus Was America's Best-Selling Car. Then It Became a Ghost.
The Honda Civic Has Killed 6,553 People. It's Still Everyone's First Recommendation.
The Toyota Camry Is the 'Safest' Car in America. It Has Killed 6,328 People.
'Altima Energy' Is Real. 4,787 People Are Dead.
The Dodge Charger Is America's Favorite Bar Car. The Toxicology Reports Prove It.
The Ford Focus Was America's Most Popular First Car. It Killed 3,046 People.
The Chevy Cobalt Was a Death Trap Before GM Even Admitted It
The Camaro Is the Second-Deadliest Sports Car in America. It's Not Even Close to First.
item.description: At 3.44 deaths per 100M VMT, the Camaro kills at half the Mustang's rate — but with a higher impairment percentage. 1,204 dead in a decade.

item.title: Nissan's "Luxury" Sedan Is Twice as Deadly as the Altima
item.description: At 5.11 deaths per 100M VMT, the Maxima is nearly 2× deadlier than the Altima — and it's not even a sports car.

item.title: Pickup Trucks Account for 1 in 5 Traffic Deaths in America
item.description: 41,593 pickup fatalities over a decade. The Silverado alone tops the entire database at 9,591. But per mile, they're safer than sedans.

item.title: The Chevrolet Impala: America's Deadliest Rental Car
item.description: At 5.0 deaths per 100M VMT and 3,774 fatalities, the fleet-favorite Impala is 2.5× deadlier than its own sibling, the Malibu.

item.title: The Ford Mustang Has the Highest Death Rate of Any Mass-Market Car in America
item.description: At 6.02 deaths per 100M VMT and 2,739 fatalities, the Mustang is the deadliest mainstream vehicle on American roads.

item.title: The Hyundai Veloster Is the Deadliest Car in America Per Mile Driven
item.description: At 8.54 deaths per 100M VMT, this economy coupe out-kills the Mustang, Camaro, and Corvette — and its drivers are comparatively sober.

item.title: The Tesla Paradox: Silicon Valley's Safety Darling Meets the Muscle Car's Worst Habit
item.description: Model Y posts 0.03 deaths per 100M VMT. Model S posts a 24% impairment rate. Same brand, different species.

item.title: How the Ford Explorer Escaped Its Own Legacy
item.description: 503 fatal crash involvements for the 2002 model year. 8 for the 2022. A 98.4% reduction.

item.title: One In Four Corvette Drivers In Fatal Crashes Is Impaired
item.description: 26.2% tested positive for alcohol or drugs — the highest of any major sports car. The Buick Park Avenue hits 31.7%.

item.title: The 261x Death Gap: How Your SUV Choice Is a Life-or-Death Decision
item.description: The fatality rate gap between the Chevrolet Tracker and the Porsche Macan is 261-fold.

item.title: The Honda Accord Has Killed More People Than the Mustang, Camaro, Corvette, and Challenger Combined
item.description: 7,102 Accord deaths vs. 4,648 for all four muscle cars. Ubiquity is its own kind of danger.

item.title: The Chevy Astro Van: Where 27% of Drivers in Fatal Crashes Were Loaded
item.description: A minivan out-drinks the Mustang. Impairment correlates with vehicle price, not vehicle type.

item.title: The Toyota Land Cruiser Paradox: Sober Drivers, Maximum Death
item.description: 3rd-lowest impairment rate. 3rd-highest death rate. The most unsettling data point in the database.

item.title: The Civic Gets Safer Every Generation; the Forte Gets Deadlier
item.description: FARS model-year data shows seven popular vehicles producing more deaths per vintage, not fewer, even as competitors in the same segments cut fatalities by 60% or more.
